$comment: Module 4 - Programming Quantum Computers question bank. One item per objective, tagged by objective_id. Grading matches apps.quizzes.views. JSON backslashes single-escaped -> one literal backslash for KaTeX.
title: Module 4 - Programming Quantum Computers

quizzes.description: Registers, gate instructions, measurement, and statevector vs sampling.

questions.text: Which best describes the quantum circuit programming model?
questions.hint: Gates are unitary/reversible; measurement is the probabilistic, irreversible step.
questions.explanation: A program allocates a register initialised to all zeros, applies an ordered list of unitary gates, and measures to obtain classical outcomes. Only measurement is irreversible.

options.text: a quantum register starts in $|0\dots0\rangle$; gates are unitary instructions applied in order; measurement samples the basis and writes classical bits
qubits hold classical bits and gates may be irreversible
all gates act on every qubit at once and can be cloned
measurement is deterministic and reversible

questions.text: In the program `h q0; cx q0 q1; measure`, what does the second instruction do?
questions.hint: `cx a b` = controlled-X with control a, target b.
questions.explanation: `cx q0 q1` applies CNOT with q0 as control and q1 as target; combined with the preceding `h q0` it prepares a Bell state.

options.text: a CNOT with control q0 and target q1
a CNOT with control q1 and target q0
a Hadamard on q1
a measurement of q0

questions.text: How do the statevector view and the sampling view of a running circuit differ?
questions.hint: Real hardware returns only samples; amplitudes need a simulator.
questions.explanation: The statevector is the full amplitude vector, available only in simulation; sampling runs many shots and reports frequencies that approach the Born-rule probabilities.

options.text: the statevector gives the exact $2^n$ complex amplitudes (simulator only); sampling gives shot counts approximating $|\langle x|\psi\rangle|^2$
they always return identical numbers
the statevector is available on real hardware but sampling is not
sampling gives amplitudes and the statevector gives probabilities

questions.text: Running `h q0; cx q0 q1` over many shots gives approximately which outcome distribution?
questions.hint: This is the Bell state $\tfrac{1}{\sqrt2}(|00\rangle+|11\rangle)$.
questions.explanation: The circuit prepares $\tfrac{1}{\sqrt2}(|00\rangle+|11\rangle)$, so the two qubits are perfectly correlated: only `00` and `11` appear, each ~50%.

options.text: ~50% `00` and ~50% `11`, essentially never `01` or `10`

quizzes.description: Bell/GHZ preparation, composition order, parameterized gates, state equality.

questions.text: Which program prepares the 3-qubit GHZ state $\tfrac{1}{\sqrt2}(|000\rangle+|111\rangle)$?
questions.hint: Hadamard the first qubit, then propagate with a CNOT chain.
questions.explanation: `h q0` makes $\tfrac{1}{\sqrt2}(|0\rangle+|1\rangle)$ on q0; the CNOT chain copies that value to q1 and q2, giving the GHZ state. Option (b) makes $|{+}{+}{+}\rangle$.

options.text: `h q0; cx q0 q1; cx q1 q2`
`h q0; h q1; h q2`
`x q0; x q1; x q2`
`cx q0 q1; cx q1 q2`

questions.text: A subcircuit $A$ is applied, then a subcircuit $B$. The overall unitary is:
questions.hint: Later-in-time operators multiply on the left.
questions.explanation: Acting on $|\psi\rangle$, first $A$ then $B$ gives $U_B U_A|\psi\rangle$. Because gates generally do not commute, the order matters.

options.text: $U_B U_A$
$U_A U_B$
$U_A + U_B$
$U_A U_B U_A$

questions.text: What is the role of a parameterized gate such as $R_x(\theta)$ in building circuits?
questions.hint: $\theta$ is a continuous knob tuned e.g. by a classical optimizer.
questions.explanation: Parameterized gates turn a fixed circuit into a family indexed by continuous angles, which variational algorithms (Module 6) optimize over.

options.text: a single circuit template with a free angle defines a continuous family of states/unitaries (the basis of variational algorithms)
it measures a qubit at angle $\theta$
it is only a global phase and has no effect
it can only take the values $0$ or $\pi$

questions.text: The programs `h q0` and `x q0; h q0` prepare the same single-qubit state.
questions.hint: Compute $H|0\rangle$ and $H(X|0\rangle)=H|1\rangle$.
questions.explanation: False. $H|0\rangle=|+\rangle$ but $H|1\rangle=|-\rangle$; the extra $X$ flips $|0\rangle\to|1\rangle$ first, giving a different (orthogonal) output.

options.text: True
False

quizzes.description: Shots, statistical error, expectation values, simulators vs samplers.

questions.text: What is a measurement 'shot', and why do repeated shots fluctuate?
questions.hint: Each shot is one random draw from the output distribution.
questions.explanation: A shot is a single execution returning one outcome; estimating probabilities from a finite number of such draws carries Monte-Carlo sampling noise.

options.text: one prepare-run-measure cycle yielding a single bitstring; finite sampling of a probabilistic outcome gives statistical fluctuations
a shot returns the exact probability, so there are no fluctuations
a shot measures all amplitudes at once
fluctuations come only from gate errors, never from sampling

questions.text: To resolve an outcome probability to a statistical error of about $\pm 0.01$, roughly how many shots are needed (order-of-magnitude, using error $\sim 1/\sqrt{N}$)?
questions.hint: Set $1/\sqrt N \approx 0.01$ and solve for $N$.
questions.explanation: $1/\sqrt N \approx \epsilon \Rightarrow N \approx 1/\epsilon^2 = 1/(0.01)^2 = 10{,}000$ shots. Halving the error would quadruple this.

questions.text: A qubit is measured in the $Z$ basis over $N=1000$ shots, giving $n_0=750$ zeros and $n_1=250$ ones. Compute $\langle Z\rangle$.
questions.hint: $\langle Z\rangle = (n_0 - n_1)/N$.

questions.text: How does an ideal statevector simulator differ from a shot-based sampler?
questions.hint: Exact amplitudes vs finite-shot frequencies.
questions.explanation: A statevector simulator computes exact amplitudes/probabilities (memory $\propto 2^n$); a sampler mimics hardware, returning frequencies with $1/\sqrt N$ fluctuations.

options.text: the statevector simulator returns exact probabilities (no sampling noise) but costs $2^n$ memory; the sampler returns a noisy histogram like real hardware
they are identical in cost and output
the sampler is exact and the statevector simulator is approximate
the statevector simulator cannot handle entanglement

quizzes.description: Transpilation, the 2^n wall, stabilizer vs statevector simulation, memory estimates.

questions.text: What does transpilation do?
questions.hint: It adapts an abstract circuit to a specific device without changing what it computes.
questions.explanation: Transpilation decomposes gates into native ones and routes two-qubit gates onto adjacent physical qubits; it alters gate count/depth but not the logical operation.

options.text: rewrites a circuit into the backend's native gate set and qubit connectivity (inserting SWAPs), preserving the logical unitary
measures the circuit to estimate its output
deletes redundant qubits to shrink the statevector
changes the logical unitary to reduce error

questions.text: Why is exact statevector simulation limited to roughly 30-50 qubits?
questions.hint: Each added qubit doubles the memory.
questions.explanation: Storing $2^n$ amplitudes means each extra qubit doubles memory; ~30 qubits already needs ~16 GB, so exact simulation stalls in the 30-50 range.

options.text: the statevector has $2^n$ complex amplitudes, so memory grows exponentially with $n$
floating-point rounding forbids more than 50 qubits
quantum gates cannot be represented for large $n$
transpilation fails above 30 qubits

questions.text: How does a stabilizer (Clifford) simulator differ from a statevector simulator?
questions.hint: Gottesman-Knill: Clifford circuits are classically efficient.
questions.explanation: Stabilizer simulators run Clifford circuits (common in error correction) at thousands of qubits in polynomial time; non-Clifford $T$ gates are what make simulation exponentially hard.

options.text: circuits built only from Clifford gates {H, S, CNOT} are simulable in polynomial time (Gottesman-Knill); adding non-Clifford $T$ gates forces exponential cost
the stabilizer simulator is always slower
the stabilizer simulator cannot represent entanglement
they are efficient for exactly the same circuits

questions.text: How many complex amplitudes are needed to store the exact statevector of a 10-qubit register?
questions.hint: The statevector has $2^n$ amplitudes.
questions.explanation: $2^{10} = 1024$ complex amplitudes. Each additional qubit doubles this count.

quizzes.description: Coding Deutsch-Jozsa and Grover, reading histograms, oracles as gate abstractions.

questions.text: A Deutsch-Jozsa program applies, in order:
questions.hint: $H^{\otimes n}$, oracle, $H^{\otimes n}$, measure.
questions.explanation: DJ Hadamards the input register and a $|-\rangle$ ancilla, applies the phase oracle $(-1)^{f(x)}$, Hadamards again, and measures: constant $f$ yields all-zeros, balanced yields anything else.

options.text: Hadamards on the input register (and $|-\rangle$ ancilla), the phase oracle, Hadamards again, then measure
the oracle, then a single Hadamard, then measure the ancilla
only Hadamards with no oracle
Grover iterations followed by an inverse QFT

questions.text: One Grover iteration in code consists of:
questions.hint: Mark, then reflect about the mean.
questions.explanation: $G = D\,O_f$: the oracle flips the marked amplitude's phase, and diffusion reflects about the uniform state, amplifying the marked amplitude. Repeat $\approx\tfrac{\pi}{4}\sqrt N$ times.

options.text: the phase oracle $O_f$ followed by the diffusion operator $D = 2|s\rangle\langle s| - I$
the diffusion operator alone, applied twice
a single Hadamard layer
an inverse QFT then a measurement

questions.text: After running Grover, how do you read the answer from the output histogram?
questions.hint: Amplitude amplification concentrates probability on the marked state.
questions.explanation: Grover drives probability onto the marked item, so it dominates the histogram; the most frequent outcome is the answer, and additional shots reduce sampling noise.

options.text: the tallest bar (most frequent bitstring) is the marked item; more shots sharpen it
the shortest bar is the answer
the answer is the average of all bitstrings
the histogram is irrelevant; only the statevector phase matters

questions.text: An oracle such as $O_f$ is an abstraction that, to run on hardware, must itself be compiled into elementary gates whose cost counts.
questions.hint: Hardware has no built-in 'oracle' instruction.
questions.explanation: True. Oracles are black boxes in the algorithm's analysis, but a real device implements them as concrete gate sequences, and that synthesis cost is part of the true resource count.

options.text: True
False
